text: feature of economic globalization, in the form in which it started to spread from the end of the Cold War, is its indefiniteness and boundlessness. There is no limit in space or time; markets remain open 24 hours a day – indeed, even this notion of ‘24 hours a day’ is clearly an inept and outdated way of conceiving of it, as if it still operates with days and hours. It would be more correct to say that time has been dissolved. The global economy is driven by the movement of resources and assets, in constant projection against an equally mobile future horizon of expectations. The ‘surplus value’ that Marx had tried to predict has been lifted out of time and place and with no reference to the materiality of objects; it is generated in a void. Expectations eventually collapse and lead people into disarray, but new ones evidently keep forming from the debris. We are seemingly caught in this perpetual liminal game. Any political attempt to ‘regulate’ it runs against not just power politics and technical challenges, but also a resignatory attitude towards the enormity of the issue, slipping out of our hands. Did we say ‘resources’? Here lies another element of liminality: those assets that move around in constant flux are to a high degree no-things: calculated values on the basis of calculated values. In his discussion of liminality, Kieran Keohane perceptively captures some of the essential characteristics of this ‘libidinal economy’, and the ‘financialization’ he fittingly describes as social pathogenesis (Keohane 2009). The sheer fact that our current economy has been described as ‘casino capitalism’ (and prophetically so by Strange in 1986) evidences the ways in which liminal play permeates the entire economic sector, making up the slippery soil beneath its self-sustained rationality. It is also one of the most visible ways in which the carnivalesque and the abstract-rational generation of value actually have come together. Bubbles in the Air: Perfect Nothingness The most down-to-earth (if it is still here) expression that frames this liminal/ libidinal economy is the constant eruption of ‘bubble economies’. Such bubbles must quite literally be understood as fragile surface entities floating around in space, rising above us, creating growth, increasing consumption, growing themselves – until they burst and the liminal nothingness falls down upon us. All bubbles burst, as children know all too well. Then we start to blow another one. By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 219 This is not just a recent epidemic. Economic historians generally concur that the first major economic bubble grew and then burst in Amsterdam in the year 1637, known as the great ‘Tulip Mania of 1637’. Incidentally, this just one year before the casino became institutionalized (and the decision to actually open the Ridotto was with all likelihood taken in 1637, when gambling was spontaneously spreading in the streets during what was turning into the quasi-permanent carnival in Venice). It was also the same year that the first opera house opened, still in Venice, near the Rialto, that Descartes published Discourse on Method, and that a series of events (including the explosion of the Scottish rebellion against Charles I) paved the way to civil war and puritanist revolution in Britain, creating a legacy. Hobbes returned to England that year, witnessing king and parliament in heated struggle, and decided that life consists of fear and greed. The first English trade-voyage venture to China was launched, challenging Spanish–Portuguese hegemony. In America, new English Puritans launched a war against the Pequot Indians, anticipating the larger genocide to come. There is an extent to which we still live in the aftermath of 1637. The tulip-bubble event was popularized in 1841 by a book written by Charles Mackay, fittingly entitled Extraordinary Popular Delusions and the Madness of Crowds. At the peak of the tulip mania some single tulip bulbs sold for more than ten times the annual income of a skilled craftsman. Then of course the prices plummeted. The tulip bulb is, after all, a poisonous onion, and onions are curiously empty at their core. Economists are greatly at odds when it comes to explaining bubble economies – and some stubbornly deny their existence. Technically speaking, an economic bubble is defined as ‘trade in high volumes at prices that are considerably at variance with intrinsic values’. Prices and ‘values’ keep going up and up, based on expectations and mimetic desire, whereas the intrinsic value stays the same – or goes down. Large stock market trends often begin and end with periods of frenzied buying (bubbles) or selling (crashes). The bubble grows and grows, until it bursts. This might sound like an innocent children’s story; it is the story of the hard realities and social epidemics of our economy. There is an acute need for social scientists to turn their hands and minds back to this madness, for it is far too serious a business to leave just to economists. This engagement must happen without taking departure from any ideological position, and certainly without positing state-control as an alternative to market forces. The workings of our economy are extremely complex and technical, but there can be no doubt that the underlying mechanisms are social and deeply anthropological, in both the empirical and the philosophical sense. Interests and Passions and the In-Between It is a widespread assumption made by economists (and not a few social and political scientists) that human beings are fundamentally driven by the rational pursuit of the satisfaction of their interests. The very nature of such interests, and Liminality and the Modern 220 how they emerge, is something that most economists – quite consciously, and very explicitly – leave others to think about. Marxists endorse the notion of ‘objective interests’, transposing the subject of such interests from the individual to the social class – and from that position as well, the question is closed before we even start to formulate it: what do human beings want? Liberal economists argue that ‘ends’ cannot be theorized or normatively accounted for
